planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic, sandy soil in full sun; Old Man Banksia is a slow-growing large shrub to small tree requiring many years to reach mature size and first flower. Like all Proteaceae, it requires excellent drainage and absolutely no phosphorus fertilizer. It is fire-adapted — the large, persistent woody cones (follicles) open to release seeds after fire. In the garden it grows more slowly without fire; the seed cones can be individually heated to release seeds. Famous in Australia as the model for 'Big Bad Banksia Men' in May Gibbs's classic Gumnut Babies stories.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic, sandy soil in full sun; Old Man Banksia is Australia's most culturally iconic banksia — famous both as a botanical landmark and as the model for the 'Big Bad Banksia Men' in May Gibbs's classic 1918 Australian children's book series 'Gumnut Babies', where the gnarled, leathery, toothy-faced spent flower cones with their hairy, follicle-covered surface became the memorable villains of Australian childhood imagination. As a living plant it is one of the most ancient-looking trees in the Australian flora — a large, rugged, slow-growing shrub to small tree with deeply fissured, rough gray bark, thick, leathery, saw-toothed (serrata) leaves with pale undersides, and in summer through autumn, large, cylindrical, cream-yellow to pale-gold flower spikes 6–8 inches long composed of thousands of individual small, densely packed flowers — the whole structure eventually developing into the large, woody, hairy-textured, persistent seed cone that inspired the 'Banksia Man' appearance. The flower spikes are excellent nectar sources for honeyeating birds, bees, and in cultivation, hummingbirds. A very long-lived tree where conditions are suitable, eventually forming gnarled, large specimens of great character.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic, sandy soil in full sun; heath banksia is one of the faster-growing and more prolific-flowering banksias — reaching flowering size in 3–5 years and producing from autumn through winter large numbers of the spectacular orange-russet flower spikes that are among the most vivid in the genus. Like all Proteaceae, absolutely no phosphorus fertilizer. Outstanding for nectar-feeding birds including hummingbirds in cultivation.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic, sandy soil in full sun; heath banksia is one of the most garden-worthy and prolific-flowering of the larger banksia species — producing from autumn through winter striking, cylindrical flower spikes 8–12 inches long in warm, vivid orange to russet-orange to orange-gold, made up of densely packed hundreds of individual small, nectar-rich flowers. The fine, needle-like leaves (resembling heather leaves — hence 'heath banksia' and the species name ericifolia) create a fine-textured, dark-green, somewhat heather-like foliage year-round, providing pleasant contrast to the large, bold flower spikes. The autumn-to-winter bloom time fills a gap in the garden calendar when few other large-flowered shrubs are at peak, and the vivid, warm orange of the flower spikes is particularly welcome in the cooler months. In cultivation the dense, nectar-rich flower spikes are enthusiastically visited by hummingbirds throughout the winter bloom period. One of the more adaptable and garden-practical banksia species for zone 9–11 gardens outside Australia, combining reasonable growth rate, generous flowering, and striking flower color. Requires the same excellent drainage and zero-phosphorus care as all Proteaceae.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in very well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy soil in full sun; Mangle's kangaroo paw is the floral emblem of Western Australia. Like all kangaroo paws it is susceptible to ink disease (Alternaria fungal spotting) in humid conditions — improve airflow and avoid overhead watering. It blooms in spring and summer. Division every 2–3 years maintains vigor and flowering. The red-and-green bicolor is the most dramatic and recognizable of the kangaroo paw species.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy soil in full sun; red-and-green kangaroo paw is the most iconic and most beautiful of all kangaroo paw species — the floral emblem of Western Australia, recognized internationally as the botanical symbol of Australian flora, and one of the most distinctive and immediately recognizable flowers in the world. The flowers are produced in clusters on upright, branching stems in an extraordinary, vivid bicolor of bright, true-red to crimson-red below (on the stem and lower floral tube) and bright emerald-green on the tubular, six-lobed, velvety upper flower — the combination of saturated red and vivid green in the dense, velvety, fur-textured flower tubes creating a striking, completely unique color contrast that is unlike any other commonly grown ornamental plant. The common name 'kangaroo paw' perfectly describes the shape — the six diverging lobes of the perianth tube spreading and curving like the toes of a kangaroo's paw. It blooms in spring and summer, attracting honeyeating birds (in Australia) and hummingbirds (in cultivation) through the highly productive nectar in the long, tubular flowers. The primary parent species of the many modern Anigozanthos hybrids. A signature plant for zone 9 California and Gulf Coast Mediterranean-style gardens.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in very well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy soil in full sun; green kangaroo paw is smaller than most other species and more susceptible to ink disease (Alternaria) in humid conditions. It blooms in spring. Division every 2 years maintains vigor. The uniformly bright-green, velvety flower tubes are unusual and striking — one of the few purely green-flowered ornamental perennials.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy soil in full sun; green kangaroo paw is the most botanically unusual of the commonly grown Anigozanthos species for a simple reason — all parts of the flower, from the stem to the velvety, six-lobed tube, are the same uniformly vivid, pure emerald-green, creating a flower that is one of the very few purely green-flowered ornamental perennials available to gardeners. True-green flowers — not pale or yellowish-green but clear, vivid, saturated emerald-green — are exceedingly rare in ornamental plants, and the kangaroo paw flower form in vivid green creates an unusual, sophisticated, somewhat shocking effect in the garden: a plant entirely of brilliant green flowers that most observers initially mistake for architectural green foliage until they look closely. Native to the coastal heathlands of southwestern Western Australia in a restricted coastal zone south of Perth, it is smaller and more compact than A. manglesii or A. flavidus, with narrow, strap-shaped leaves and smaller flowering stems. For florists and garden designers seeking the unexpected and rare — a purely green-flowered plant for sophisticated color compositions — green kangaroo paw is one of the very few options.

subcategory: Australian botanical novelty with triggered pollination

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in moist, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy soil in full sun to part shade; grass trigger plant is native to the sandy, peaty, often seasonally moist soils of southeastern Australia. The trigger mechanism — the column (fused style and stamens) that snaps to deposit pollen on visiting insects — can be observed and reset by gently touching a flower; it will snap back within a few hours. The plant is partially insectivorous, secreting sticky glands on the stem that trap small insects.

directions: Plant in moist, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy soil in full sun to part shade; grass trigger plant is one of the most remarkable and botanically interesting of all commonly grown ornamental plants — a botanical curiosity on par with the Venus flytrap in its unusual adaptation. Each flower of Stylidium contains a 'trigger' — the column, a structure formed by the fusion of style and stamens — that is held under tension and, when touched by a visiting insect seeking nectar, snaps rapidly across the flower in a powerful, spring-like mechanism, depositing pollen onto the insect's body (or collecting pollen from a pollen-carrying visitor). This triggered snap is one of the fastest movements in the plant kingdom — occurring in milliseconds — and can be reset: after firing, the column slowly resets to the cocked position over a few hours, ready to fire again. The flowers are small, pink to rose-pink, five-petaled, and produced in many-flowered racemes on stems above the narrow, grass-like (graminifolium = grass-leaved) basal leaf rosette in spring and early summer. Additionally, the glandular-sticky stem hairs trap small insects — contributing to the plant's partially insectivorous nutrition. A plant that combines attractive appearance with a genuinely dramatic and demonstrable botanical mechanism that can be shown to observers in the garden.
